coredata.dc:title: Dry reforming of CH4 CO2 in AC rotating gliding arc discharge: Effect of electrode structure and gas parameters
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2018 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Na
Sun, Danfeng
Xia, Yun
Shang, Kefeng
Wang, Bing
Jiang, Nan
Li, Jie
Wu, Yan
coredata.dc:description: Conversion of CH4 and CO2 into synthesis gas has been performed in a rotating gliding arc discharge (RGAD) reactor driven by a high frequency AC power. Influence of electrode structure and gas parameters on RGAD plasma CH4 CO2 conversion and energy efficiency of the process was investigated. Summit angle of internal electrode is an important parameter affecting the stability of the reaction. Internal electrode with summit angle of 45° is more favorable for CH4 and CO2 conversions. The energy efficiencies increase by 25% and 22% than those in RGAD involving internal electrode with summit angle of 30° and 60°, respectively. Longer external electrode improves the arc extension leading to an increase of 17% and 25% in CO2 and CH4 conversion at the applied voltage of 11 kV. Feed flow rate has significant impact on the CO2 and CH4 conversion, while low CO2/CH4 ratio inhibits rotating gliding arc extension due to carbon-black deposition. A large number of graphene quantum dots have been observed to generate on the lamellar ley of the carbon-black. Maximum energy efficiency of 3.9 mmol/kJ is achieved at feed flow rate of 3 L/min and CO2/CH4 ratio of 3:2 in this work.
coredata.dcterms:subject: AC rotating gliding arc
Electrode structure
Gas parameters
Dry reforming of CH4
Energy efficiency

Effect of CO2 to CH4 ratio

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic of RGAD experimental setup (a) and RGAD reactor (b).
Internal electrodes with summit angles of 60°, 45° and 30°.
Different external-electrode lengths.
Effect of internal-electrode summit angle on conversion rates of CO2 and CH4. (The total flow rate 2L/min, CO2 to CH4 ratio 3:2).
Effect of internal-electrode summit angle on selectivity of CO, H2 (a) and CxHy (C2H2, C2H4, C2H6) (b).
Effect of internal-electrode summit angle on energy efficiency and SED.
Effect of external electrode length on conversion rates of CO2 and CH4. (The summit angle: 45°, CO2 to CH4 ratio: 3:2).
Effect of external electrode length on selectivity of (a) CO, H2 and (b) CxHy (C2H2, C2H4, C2H6).
Effect of external electrode length on energy efficiency and SED.
Effect of flow rate on conversion rate of CO2 and CH4. (The summit angle: 45°; CO2 to CH4 ratio: 3:2; applied voltage: 8 kV)
Effect of flow rate on (a) selectivity of CO and H2 (b) CxHy.
Effect of flow rate on energy efficiency and yield rate of H2 and CO.
Effect of CO2 to CH4 ratio on conversion rate of CO2 and CH4 (The summit angle: 45°; total flow rate: 3L/min; applied voltage: 8 kV).
Effect of CO2 to CH4 ratio on (a) selectivity of CO and H2 (b).
Effect of CO2 to CH4 ratio on yield rate of H2 as well as CO and energy efficiency.
SEM images of carbon-black.
TEM images of carbon-black.
Raman spectra of carbon-black (532 nm excitation) shows contributions of both SP2 (G-band at 1580 cm−1) and SP3 carbons (D-band at 1344 cm−1).
Optimum electrode structure and gas parameters for the best energy efficiency.
Comparison of the conversion and energy efficiency of dry reforming with different plasmas.

head.ce:title: Dry reforming of CH4 CO2 in AC rotating gliding arc discharge: Effect of electrode structure and gas parameters

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Communications Research Institute of Liaoning Province

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Civil Engineering and Architecture, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou 310058, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Civil Engineering and Architecture

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Conversion of CH4 and CO2 into synthesis gas has been performed in a rotating gliding arc discharge (RGAD) reactor driven by a high frequency AC power. Influence of electrode structure and gas parameters on RGAD plasma CH4 CO2 conversion and energy efficiency of the process was investigated. Summit angle of internal electrode is an important parameter affecting the stability of the reaction. Internal electrode with summit angle of 45° is more favorable for CH4 and CO2 conversions. The energy efficiencies increase by 25% and 22% than those in RGAD involving internal electrode with summit angle of 30° and 60°, respectively. Longer external electrode improves the arc extension leading to an increase of 17% and 25% in CO2 and CH4 conversion at the applied voltage of 11 kV. Feed flow rate has significant impact on the CO2 and CH4 conversion, while low CO2/CH4 ratio inhibits rotating gliding arc extension due to carbon-black deposition. A large number of graphene quantum dots have been observed to generate on the lamellar ley of the carbon-black. Maximum energy efficiency of 3.9 mmol/kJ is achieved at feed flow rate of 3 L/min and CO2/CH4 ratio of 3:2 in this work.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, there has been a growing concern over the problem of global warming caused by greenhouse gas emissions such as carbon dioxide (CO2), which contributes 80% of the greenhouse effect [1–5]. The burning of fossil fuels is the main source of CO2. Moreover, fossil fuels are non-renewable source, and searching for sustainable clean energy sources is another significant concern in addition to global warming [6].

Considering that CO2 is a cheap and abundant feed stock, one promising strategy for reducing CO2 emission is to recycle CO2 into carbon monoxide (CO) as liquid fuel synthesis [7]. However, CO2 is an inert and stable gas because its OCO bond energy is up to 532 kJ/mol [8]. Even under the condition of the temperature rising to 2000 K, the decomposition rate of CO2 molecular into CO and O2 is only 1.8% [9].

Methane (CH4), a kind of greenhouse gas, is a main component of natural gas and biogas. Combining CH4 with CO2, which is usually called dry reforming of CH4, can produce CO and hydrogen (H2), as shown in Eq. (1) [6,8,10,11]. The dry reforming reaction not only consumes greenhouse gases but also produces clean energy, which has attracted increasing interest. Catalysis is a broad field that an organic compound either in gas or liquid state is transformed to another one utilizing an electron relay mechanism on a catalyst surface via a redox reaction [12]. There are many researches regarding thermal catalytic technologies for CH4 conversion towards H2 that have been reported in the past few years [13–18]. However, the harsh conditions such as high temperature as well as high pressure cause high energy cost. Furthermore, the catalyst is very easy to inactivate due to the carbon-black deposition and high temperature condition.
(1)
C
H 4
+ C
O 2
→ 2 CO + 2
H 2
,
Δ
H
298 K
= 2.05 ×
10
− 19
Ws
/
(
CO +
H 2
)
molecule

Non-thermal plasma can initiate difficult or even impossible chemical processes, which are far from thermodynamic equilibrium under room temperature and atmospheric pressure conditions [19–23]. In this case, most of the energy is consumed to produce high-energy electrons rather than heat gas, and they can activate the gas molecules generating active species. Consequently, high energy efficiency is reflected in the process of stimulating chemical reaction.

Several methods have been employed to produce non-thermal plasma for dry reforming of CH4 CO2, such as corona discharge [24–26], dielectric barrier discharge (DBD) [27–34], microwave discharge [23,35–39] and gliding arc discharge (GAD) [6,20,40–45]. The corona discharge is difficult to obtain larger plasma volume, which limits its industrial application [46,47]. DBD plasma exhibits good performance for CH4 and CO2 conversion under lower total feed flow rate, which makes it less capable of reforming and energy utilization. Microwave discharge achieves the highest energy efficiency, but the very specific conditions (supersonic gas and reduced pressure of about 100–200 Torr) make it to be incompatible with the economic requirements for practical application. Compared with these discharge modes, the GAD plasma can produce thermal plasma and non-thermal plasma simultaneously [48]. The maximum value of electron density in the blade GAD zone can reach 2.2 × 1023 m−3 when the arc exceeds the restricted zone to the free jet zone [6]. In addition, the whole discharge process presents the significant characteristics of non-thermal plasma without very high temperature (2000–4000 K) because most energy (75%–80%) is consumed in the non-thermal plasma phase. The character of GAD plasma could enable thermodynamically unfavourable chemical reactions such as exhausted gas degradation and conversion of greenhouses to occur at atmospheric pressure and low temperatures.

The rotating gliding arc discharge (RGAD) plasma is generated in a turbulent gas flow at atmospheric pressure and is growing interest in dry reforming of CH4 CO2 [49–51]. In comparison to traditional GAD plasma, it has the advantages of long resistance time, large plasma volume and low energy loss. The formation of stable RGAD undergoes two gliding modes. At the moment of the narrowest gap between internal and external electrodes breaking down, the arcs begin to generate, namely breakdown gliding mode. Afterwards, the arcs slide rotationally along the surfaces of between internal and external electrodes driven by the rotating flow until stable arcs are formed, namely steady arc gliding mode.

Arc length and sliding space are the main factors affecting the GRAD plasma production, which are related to the electrode structure. Longer arc length leads to higher discharge power and is more favorable for chemical reaction, while increasing sliding space can contribute to enhance the energy utilization. Gas parameters such as total feed flow rate and CO2/CH4 ratio have significant effect on RGAD plasma CH4 CO2 conversion and energy efficiency of the process.

In this work, a RGAD reactor driven by a high frequency AC power was developed for dry reforming of CH4 CO2. Effects of internal electrode summit angle, external electrode length, total feed flow rate and CO2/CH4 ratio were well investigated in terms of CH4 and CO2 conversion, yield of H2 and CO, and energy efficiency to understand the relative significance of these parameters on RGAD plasma CH4 CO2 conversion. To get a better understanding of carbon-black formation, characterization of carbon-black is also investigated.

The schematic of RGAD experimental setup in this work is shown in Fig. 1 (a). The rotating arc is generated by connecting RGAD reactor to a high frequency AC power (CTP-2000K) with a variable frequency of 5–11 kHz. In this work, the discharge frequency remains constant at 5 kHz. Discharge voltage and current are recorded by a four-channel oscilloscope (Tektronix TDS 2014) via a high voltage probe (Tektronix P6015A) and a current probe (Tektronix P6021A), respectively. The mixture of pure CO2 (purity 99.999%) and pure CH4 (purity 99.99%) is used as the feed gas, and the feed gas flow rate is controlled by mass flow controllers (Beijing, MFC, D07-7). The feed gas is fed into RGAD reactor via a spiral glass tube with inner diameter of 2 mm. The gas products of the reaction are detected on-line with a gas chromatography (Shanghai Tianmei, GC7900).

The detail of RGAD reactor configuration is shown in Fig. 1(b). The reactor is composed of two stainless electrodes, a spiral glass tube and a glass cover. The internal electrode with cone-shaped structure (diameter 48 mm) is connected to the power supply. The external circular-cylinder electrode (diameter 36 mm, thickness 3 mm) is connected to the ground. The narrowest gap between the internal and external electrodes was 2 mm.
The diagrams of internal-electrode with summit angles of 60°, 45° and 30° are shown in Fig. 2 . The external electrodes are all 20 mm longer than the internal electrodes.

The diagrams of different external electrode lengths are shown in Fig. 3 . The summit angles are both set as 45°. The length of external electrode is 20 mm higher or 20 mm lower than the internal electrode.

The CO2 conversion rate (C CO2), CH4 conversion rate (C CH4), H2 selectivity (S H2), CO selectivity (S CO), CxHy (C2H2, C2H4, C2H6) selectivity (S CxHy), energy efficiency (E), specific energy density (SED), yield rate of H2 and CO are defined as follows.
(2)
C
C
O 2
( % )
=
(
1 −

O 2
output
C

input
)
× 100

C
C

( % )
=
(

C
H 4
output
C

)
× 100
(4)
S
H 2
( % )
=
H 2
produced
2 ×
C
C
H 4
× C
H 4
input
× 100
(5)
S
CO
( % )
=
CO
produced
C
C
H 4
× C
H 4
input +
C

O 2
× C
O 2
input

(6)
S
C x
H y
( % )
=
x ×
C x
H y
produced
C
H 4
converted + C
O 2
converted
× 100
(7)
E
(
mmol / kJ
)
=
C
H 4
converted + C
O 2
converted
Power
input
(8)
S E D
(
kJ / L
)
=
Power
input
Q
(9)
Yield
rate
of
H 2
( % )
=
H 2
produced
(
mol / s
)
2 × C
H 4
input
(
mol / s
)
× 100
(10)
Yield
rate
of
CO
( % )
=
CO

(
mol / s
)
C
H 4
input
(
mol / s
)
+ C
O 2
input
(
mol / s
)
× 100
Power input is calculated with the product of voltage and current on the power supply control panel. Q is total feed flow rate of CO2 and CH4 in the reactor at standard atmospheric pressure.

The morphologies and structure of the carbon-black were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, S-4800, Hitachi) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI-Tecnai G2 F30). The Raman spectra was carried out using a Raman spectrometer (DXR Microscope, Thermo Fisher) with 532 nm excitation wavelength.

Internal-electrode summit angle is a key factor that influences the sliding space and the length of the arc. Effect of internal-electrode summit angle on conversion rates of CO2 and CH4 at different applied voltages is presented in Fig. 4 . With the increase of applied voltage, the discharge performance is reinforced, providing more electrons to initiate the reactions for collision between CO2 and CH4 [52,53]. The conversion rates of CO2 and CH4 all exhibit linearly increase with applied voltage as the internal-electrode summit angle changing from 30° to 60°. At the same applied voltage, increasing the summit angle from 30° to 45° favors CO2 and CH4 conversion which decreases as the summit angle further increasing from 45° to 60°. Arc length is closely related to arc energy [54]. The longer arc length leads to larger discharge power, and is more favorable for chemical reaction. Compared with internal electrode with summit angle of 45°, the summit angel of 60° leads to smaller sliding space of the arc, resulting in restricted extension of the arc. Although the sliding space of the arc at summit angle of 30° is larger than that at summit angle of 45°, the energy consumption in the arc sliding process increases, causing shorter rotating time of the arc at the top of the electrode. Accordingly, internal electrode with summit angle of 45° exhibited the best performance for stable and large-area RGAD plasma generation, and shows the best performance in dry reforming of CH4 CO2.

Effect of internal-electrode summit angle on product selectivity is shown in Fig. 5 . CO and H2 are main products of CH4 CO2 conversion, the selectivities of which are much higher than gas hydrocarbon by-products. When the summit angle increases from 30° to 60°, selectivities of syngas as well as by-products are not affected significantly. It should be noted that the CO selectivity increases but the H2 selectivity decreases as the applied voltage increases. Higher applied voltage produces stronger electric field, which greatly benefits the conversion of CO2 and CH4 molecules into CO and H2. However, during the discharge process, the reverse water gas reaction shown in Eq. (11) is easily achieved. The water vapor appears on the inner wall of the glass cover, leading to the decrease of H2 selectivity. C2H2, C2H4 and C2H6 are observed as gas hydrocarbon by-products and the maximum selectivity of main by-product C2H2 is only 4.3%.
(11)
H 2
+ C
O 2
→ CO +
H 2
O

Energy efficiency and SED can represent the energy consumption of the reaction. Fig. 6 shows the energy efficiency and SED vs. applied voltage using internal electrodes with different summit angles. As the summit angle increases from 30° to 45°, both the energy efficiency and SED increase but decrease obviously as the summit angle increases from 45° to 60°. As the summit angle of the internal electrode is 30°, longer sliding length and larger sliding space of the arc are observed comparing with those using internal electrode of summit angle 45°. However, under the same applied voltage, the discharge power is not enough to support the arc sliding to the top of the electrode, so the arc only slides at the upper part of the internal electrode, resulting in lower energy utilization. As the summit angle of the internal electrode is 60°, the electrode length is shorter than that using internal electrode of summit angle 45° and a faster gas velocity at the top of the electrode has been obtained, causing the arc to break easily during sliding process. Most of the energy would rewind the air gap to produce a new arc, leading to reduced energy efficiency and SED. At the summit angle of 45°, the maximum energy efficiency is obtained as 3.15 mmol/kJ, increasing by 25% and 22% compared to those at summit angle of 30° and 60°, respectively, which is attributed to the highest conversion rates of CO2 and CH4. In addition, although the SED increases with the increase of the applied voltage, the energy efficiency decreases significantly. It indicates that increasing the energy density of the injected reactor within a certain voltage range can't improve the energy efficiency. Taking into consideration of conversion rate and energy efficiency, 45° is suggested as suitable summit angle for internal electrode.

The length of the external electrode can directly influence the slide of the cathode and anode spots, thus affecting the length of the arc. Effect of external electrode length on conversion rates of CO2 and CH4 is investigated and the results are shown in Fig. 7 . The arc length relates to RGAD plasma density which increases with the length of the external electrode, and longer arc length benefits CO2 and CH4 conversion [54]. Under applied voltage of 11 kV, the maximum CO2 and CH4 conversion rates are obtained under longer external electrode, which increase by 17% and 25% compared to the shorter external electrode, respectively. In case of the shorter external electrode, the arc is easy to disconnect because of no attachment points on the inner wall of the external electrode when the arc slides to the top of the electrode, thus reducing the arc length compared with the longer external electrode. Accordingly, the longer external electrode is more conducive to dry reforming of CH4 CO2.

Fig. 8 shows the effect of external electrode length on product selectivity for dry reforming of CH4 CO2. The CO and H2 selectivities with longer external electrode are a little higher than those with shorter external electrode. This is related to the high conversion rates of CH4 and CO2 using longer external electrode. The effect of external electrode length on gas hydrocarbon by-products selectivity is not distinct possibly because of low production of the by-products.

Fig. 9 shows the influence of external electrode length on energy efficiency and SED. The RGAD reactor using longer external electrode obtain higher energy efficiency and SED than using shorter external electrode, indicating good performance of using longer external electrode for making the best use of discharge energy in dry reforming of CH4 CO2. Although higher applied voltages are beneficial to improve the conversion rate, the energy efficiency shows a decrease trend of 0.27 mmol/kJ·kV−1 and meanwhile the SED shows an increase trend of 0.83 kJ/L·kV−1 under longer external electrode. The highest energy efficiency of approximately 3.15 mmol/kJ is obtained with the longer external electrode, increasing 0.15 mmol/kJ compared to the shorter external electrode at applied voltage of 8 kV. In case of using external electrode with its length shorter than the inner electrode, when the arc slides to the top of the inner electrode, the external electrode can't provide attachment points on the inner wall for gliding arc, causing arc easy break. On the other hand, the airflow diffuses seriously to the outside of the reactor, which weakens the gas velocity and restrains the extension of the arc. By contrast, the external electrode with its length longer than the inner electrode can ensure the arc rotates steadily at the top of the inner electrode, which lengthens the arc length effectively and thus contributes to enhancing energy efficiency. Therefore, the reactor composing of internal electrode with summit angle of 45° and longer external electrode is suggested as a suitable reactor structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of total feed flow rate
ce:section.ce:para: In RGAD plasma, flow rate is an important factor in the formation of rotating sliding arc, and has a significant effect on the residence time within the rotating gliding arc zone, which can affect the performance of the plasma [55]. The influence of total feed flow rate on conversion rates of CO2 and CH4 is shown in Fig. 10 . Under lower total feed flow rates (from 2 to 3 L/min), conversion rates of CO2 and CH4 increase with the total feed flow rate. Although increasing the flow rate can reduce the residence time of feed gas, the arc length is extended efficiently that is more significant for enhancing the conversion rate. However, under higher total feed flow rate (from 3 to 4 L/min), conversion rates of CO2 and CH4 decrease significantly. On one hand, the residence time of feed gas within the rotating gliding arc zone decreases deeply with increasing total feed flow rate, reducing the opportunity that CO2 and CH4 molecules break down into active species such as O· as well as H·. On the other hand, higher energy is required to maintain the arc rotating on top of the electrode stably under higher total feed flow rate. Nevertheless, the energy of the power supply keeps constant at a fixed applied voltage, leading to easily broken arc under higher flow rate. As a result, the total feed flow rate influence not only the reaction of RGAD plasma with feed gas but also the stable RGAD formation. In the present study, 3 L/min is found as optimal total feed gas flow.

The product selectivities for CO2 and CH4 under different feed flow rates are shown in Fig. 11 . With the increase of the total feed flow rate, both the CO and H2 selectivities decrease, the selectivities towards C2H4 and C2H6 increase slightly, while that of C2H2 increases and then decreases. Obviously lower residence time favors the generation of C2H4 and C2H6. C2H2 selectivity is higher than C2H4 and C2H6 and the maximum value is 4.7%.

Higher total feed flow rates are not beneficial to improve CO2 and CH4 conversion, but it is conducive to enhance energy efficiency in a certain feed flow range, as shown in Fig. 12 . The energy efficiency increases significantly with the feed flow rate increasing from 2 to 3 L/min and increases slightly as the feed flow rate further increasing to 4 L/min. With the increase of total feed flow rate, the amount of feed gas involving reforming increases and more amounts of feed gas molecules can be decomposed [56]. Discharge energy is able to be more utilized for feed gas conversion, especially total feed flow rate of 3 L/min, under the condition of which the maximum H2 and CO yield rates of 3.1% and 15.9% are also obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CO2 to CH4 ratio
ce:section.ce:para: During dry reforming of CH4 CO2 process of RGAD plasma, the carbon-black is gradually produced as the content of CH4 increases. Carbon-black deposing on the surface of the electrodes can seriously affect the discharge stability, thus influencing the conversion effect. Fig. 13 shows the effect of the CO2 to CH4 ratio on CO2 and CH4 conversion in case of total feed flow rate of 3 L/min at the applied voltage of 8 kV. The result shows that CO2 conversion decreases significantly with the CO2 to CH4 ratio decreasing from 4:1 to 2:3. The decrease in the molar ratio of CO2 to CH4 results in less CO2 molecule in the feed gas which reduces the opportunity of CO2 decomposition. Conversely, the CH4 conversion enhances with the increase of CH4 content. In the case of lower CO2 to CH4 ratio (from 3:2 to 2:3), large amount of carbon-black is produced seriously. CH4 decomposition is inhibited, leading to its conversion rate hardly changed.

The product selectivities are shown in Fig. 14 . The H2 selectivity increases by 20% while the CO selectivity decreases by 35% as the CO2 to CH4 ratio decrease from 4:1 to 2:3. Carbon-black is produced and attached to the surface of electrodes when CO2 to CH4 ratio is lower than 3:2, which decreases the CO selectivity. In addition, the increase of CH4 content favors the reverse water gas reaction to produce water while the production of H2 would be suppressed, resulting in slight increase of H2 selectivity. With the decrease of CO2 to CH4 ratio, the selectivites of gas hydrocarbon by-products enhances correspondingly and C2H2 selectivity is much higher than those of C2H4 and C2H6.

Effect of CO2 to CH4 ratio on yield rates of H2 and CO as well as energy efficiency is shown in Fig. 15 . At the CO2 to CH4 ratio of 4:1, the highest CO yield rate of 18.4% is obtained, while the H2 yield rate is the lowest. The maximum H2 yield rate of approximately 7% is obtained at CO2 to CH4 ratio of 2:3. However, the maximum energy efficiency of 3.9 mmol/kJ is obtained at CO2 to CH4 ratio of 3:2. In order to ensure stable gliding arc discharge and fully utilization of discharge energy, CO2 to CH4 ratio of 3:2 is suggested as the optimal ratio.

To get a better understanding of carbon-black formation, carbon-black has been characterized by SEM and TEM as results shown in Figs. 16 and 17
, respectively. Carbon-black with crimp lamellar structure is observed by SEM characterization. TEM results exhibit that a large number of nanoparticles distribute on the lamellar layer of the carbon-black. As shown in Fig. 17(d) (HR-TEM), the diameter of the nanoparticle is about 2 nm and the lattice is hexagonal shape, which is regarded as graphene quantum dots. Raman spectra can be used to analyze the structure of molecules. The Raman spectra of carbon-black is shown in Fig. 18 . The characteristic peak located at around 1344 cm−1 is attributed to the D-band, associated with the disordered structural form of crystalline carbon species, while the peak located at around 1580 cm−1 is related to G-band, corresponding to the graphitic carbon with high degree of crystallinity, order and symmetry. The D-band and G-band confirm that the carbon-black material is graphite. Carbon-black with lamellar structure is further proved by the peak around 2660 cm−1 (G′-band), which is consistent with SEM and TEM results. The 2904 cm−1 peak is the superposition of D-band and D′-band, indicating defects and poor lattice existing in carbon-black. Large lattice-defect degree in carbon-black material is found because the intensity of D-band is higher than that of G-band (ID/IG = 1.72).

In the above work, effects of internal electrode summit angle, the external electrode length, total feed flow rate and CO2 to CH4 ratio were well investigated in terms of CH4 and CO2 conversion, yield of H2 and CO and energy efficiency. Optimum electrode structure and gas parameters for the best energy efficiency are listed in Table 1 . Under the operating conditions as shown in Table 1, the best energy efficiency of 3.9 mmol/kJ was achieved.

Comparison of conversion rate and energy efficiency of CH4 CO2 dry reforming with different discharge modes are shown in Table 2 . The microwave plasma exhibited high feed conversions with an energy efficiency of 1.56 mmol/kJ, however, the total feed flow rate was only 0.2 L/min. The pulse corona discharge and DBD plasmas driven by high frequency power both showed low energy efficiencies. DBD plasma driven by 50 Hz AC power exhibited higher energy efficiency than GAD but the total flow rate was much lower than that of GAD. In our previous work, energy efficiency of 2.21 mmol/kJ under the total feed flow rate of 1.2 L/min was obtained using traditional GAD plasma. In the present study, the RGAD plasma driven by a high frequency AC power exhibited the best energy efficiency under the conditions of power frequency 5 kHz, CO2:CH4 3:2 and total flow rate 3 L/min, suggesting good energy utilization for dry reforming of CH4 CO2.

Dry reforming of CH4 CO2 in RGAD plasma at atmosphere pressure and ambient temperature was investigated in this work. The summit angle of internal electrode determined the sliding space of the arc and RGAD plasma generated using internal electrode with summit angle of 45° exhibited the best performance on feed gas conversion. External electrode length was a key factor that influenced the arc length. The arc of longer length was obtained using longer external electrode that contributed to generate RGAD plasma of larger density. Both feed gas conversion and the energy efficiency were higher than those using shorter external electrode. The increase of the total feed flow rate led to reduction of the feed residence time but extending the arc length within a certain flow range. At total feed flow rate of 3 L/min, RGAD plasma exhibited best performance for CH4 and CO2 conversion. With the decrease of CO2/CH4 ratio, the carbon-black with crimp lamellar structure was gradually produced and graphene quantum dots were observed to distribute on the lamellae layer of carbon-black. Carbon-black affected the discharge stability seriously, thus inhibiting the CO2 and CH4 conversion. Under the condition of optimal CO2 to CH4 ratio 3:2, no carbon-black was produced and the maximum energy efficiency of 3.9 mmol/kJ was obtained.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Joint Funds of the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. U1462105, and the Liaoning Provincial Natural Science Foundation (2015020610).
